paper_title: Implicit bias produces neural scaling laws in learning curves, from perceptrons to deep networks
paper_summary: This study investigates how performance curves of neural networks evolve throughout training rather than only at convergence. By examining a range of architectures—CNNs, ResNets, and Vision Transformers—on datasets such as MNIST, CIFAR‑10, and CIFAR‑100, the authors discover two novel dynamical scaling laws linked to norm‑based complexity measures that govern the decay of test error. These laws, when combined, recover the well‑known power‑law relationship observed at the end of training, providing a unified view across different models. The authors also offer a theoretical derivation for a single‑layer perceptron trained with logistic loss, showing that the same scaling behaviours arise from the implicit bias of gradient‑based optimisation. The results demonstrate that scaling laws are not merely asymptotic artefacts but persist throughout the training trajectory, offering richer insights into the interplay of model capacity, data size, and optimisation dynamics. This deeper understanding can guide more informed hyper‑parameter choices and architectural design, while also advancing the theoretical foundation of deep learning.
relevance: By revealing that scaling relationships hold at all stages of training, the paper provides a more fine‑grained tool for predicting model performance early in a training run. It bridges empirical observations with a concrete analytical model, highlighting how implicit bias shapes learning curves. These findings open avenues for designing adaptive training schedules that exploit early‑stage scaling to accelerate convergence. Future work could extend the theory to unsupervised or reinforcement learning settings and explore whether similar dynamical laws exist for other optimisation algorithms.
